course_info.title: Programming for Games
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 3 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Variables, Data Types, and the Programming Mindset
learning_objectives: Declare and initialize variables of common data types (int, float, string, bool)
Explain the difference between statically typed and dynamically typed languages
Write a simple program that takes input, stores it, and produces output
readings: Think Like a Programmer by V. Anton Spraul, Ch. 1
C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Welcome to Programming for Games. This course teaches programming from the ground up with a focus on the patterns and problems you will encounter in game development. No prior coding experience is assumed.
key_concepts: What a variable is: a named container in memory that holds a value
Primitive data types: int (whole numbers), float/double (decimal numbers), string (text), bool (true/false)
Variable declaration and assignment: type name = value;
Type safety: why the compiler rejects int x = "hello";
Console input and output as the simplest form of player interaction
lecture_notes.demonstration: Writing a text-based number guessing game that uses variables, input, and output in C#
setup_requirements: Visual Studio Code with C# extension installed

review.value: A named location in memory that stores a value of a specific type; the name is chosen by the programmer, the type determines what operations are valid
int stores whole numbers, float stores decimals with ~7 digits precision, string stores text, bool stores true or false

learning_objectives: Write if, else if, and else statements to branch program execution
Use comparison operators (==, !=, <, >, <=, >=) and logical operators (&&, ||, !)
Implement a simple decision tree for a text-based game scenario
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 3
Head First C# by Andrew Stellman, Ch. 2

lecture_notes.introduction: Programs that only execute line by line from top to bottom are not very interesting. Conditionals let your program make decisions, which is the foundation of every interactive experience.
key_concepts: Boolean expressions: evaluating conditions to true or false
if statement: execute a block only when a condition is true
else and else-if: providing alternative branches
Comparison operators: ==, !=, <, >, <=, >= for comparing values
Logical operators: && (AND), || (OR), ! (NOT) for combining conditions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a text adventure room with branching paths: 'You see a door and a window. What do you do?' using nested conditionals
setup_requirements: Starter code file (provided on course page)

review.value: A control structure that executes different code blocks depending on whether a boolean expression evaluates to true or false
&& requires both conditions true, || requires at least one true, ! inverts the result; used to build complex decision logic

learning_objectives: Write for, while, and do-while loops for repeated execution
Use loops to process collections of game data (enemy lists, inventory items)
Identify and fix common loop errors: off-by-one errors and infinite loops
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 4
Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Introduction (online)

lecture_notes.introduction: Games are built on repetition: every frame is a loop iteration, every enemy in a wave is processed in a loop, every item in an inventory is checked in a loop. Mastering loops is essential.
key_concepts: for loop: when you know how many iterations in advance (for int i = 0; i < count; i++)
while loop: when the number of iterations depends on a runtime condition
do-while loop: guaranteed to execute at least once, checked at the end
Loop control: break (exit immediately) and continue (skip to next iteration)
Common pitfalls: off-by-one errors (< vs <=), infinite loops (forgetting to update the condition)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Spawning a wave of 10 enemies in a console game simulation using a for loop, then using a while loop to run the combat round until all enemies or the player are defeated
setup_requirements: Enemy data file (JSON array of enemy stats, provided)

review.value: Best when iteration count is known: initializer, condition, and increment are declared together, making the loop's scope explicit
The most common loop bug; occurs when the loop runs one too many or one too few times due to using < instead of <= or starting at 1 instead of 0

learning_objectives: Declare and call functions with parameters and return values
Apply the Single Responsibility Principle to function design
Refactor a long procedural program into well-named functions
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 5
Clean Code by Robert C. Martin, Ch. 3

lecture_notes.introduction: As programs grow, putting everything in one block of code becomes unmaintainable. Functions let you name a reusable block of logic, making your code readable, testable, and organized.
key_concepts: Function declaration: return type, name, parameters, and body
Parameters and arguments: passing data into a function
Return values: getting data back from a function
Void functions: performing actions without returning a value
Single Responsibility Principle: each function should do one thing and do it well
lecture_notes.demonstration: Refactoring a 100-line combat calculator into named functions: CalculateDamage(), ApplyArmor(), CheckCritical(), DisplayResult()

review.value: A named, reusable block of code that takes input (parameters), performs a specific task, and optionally returns output (return value)
Each function should have one reason to change; if you cannot describe what a function does without using 'and,' it likely does too much

learning_objectives: Define a class with fields, properties, constructors, and methods
Instantiate objects and call their methods
Model a game entity (Player, Enemy, Item) as a class
readings: C# Programming Yellow Book by Rob Miles, Ch. 6-7
Head First C# by Andrew Stellman, Ch. 5

lecture_notes.introduction: Object-oriented programming lets you model game entities as self-contained units that combine data and behavior. Every character, item, and system in a modern game is an object.
key_concepts: Class: a blueprint defining the fields (data) and methods (behavior) of an object
Object: a specific instance created from a class blueprint
Constructor: a special method that initializes an object when it is created
Encapsulation: hiding internal data behind public methods and properties
The 'this' keyword: referring to the current object instance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a Player class (health, attack, defense, name) and an Enemy class, then running a turn-based combat sequence between two objects

review.value: A class is the blueprint (Player), an object is a specific instance built from that blueprint (player1 with 100 HP and 'Aria' as name)
Restricting direct access to an object's internal data and exposing it only through controlled methods, preventing invalid states like negative health

class_schedule.topic: The Game Loop, Collision Detection, and Event Handling
learning_objectives: Implement a fixed-timestep game loop that updates and renders at consistent intervals
Detect axis-aligned bounding box (AABB) collisions between two rectangular game objects
Handle keyboard and mouse input using an event-driven model
readings: Game Programming Patterns by Robert Nystrom, Ch. 9 (Game Loop)
Game Physics Engine Development by Ian Millington, Ch. 1

lecture_notes.introduction: The game loop is the heartbeat of every interactive application. Today we bring together everything you have learned to build the fundamental runtime architecture of a game.
key_concepts: The game loop: while(running) { processInput(); update(dt); render(); }
Fixed vs. variable timestep: why fixed timestep prevents physics instability
Delta time (dt): the elapsed time between frames, used to make movement frame-rate independent
AABB collision detection: checking if two axis-aligned rectangles overlap by comparing min/max coordinates on each axis
Event handling: polling input state each frame versus responding to discrete events (key down, key up, mouse click)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a simple 2D game with a player rectangle that moves with keyboard input, collects coin rectangles (AABB collision), and displays a score, all driven by a game loop
setup_requirements: MonoGame or Raylib-cs NuGet package installed for 2D rendering
Keyboard and mouse for input testing

review.value: The continuous cycle of processInput(), update(), render() that runs every frame; the fundamental architecture pattern of all real-time games
Two rectangles overlap if and only if: A.left < B.right AND A.right > B.left AND A.top < B.bottom AND A.bottom > B.top

quizzes.topic: Variables, Data Types, Conditionals, and Loops

questions.question: Write a C# for loop that iterates through an array of integers called 'enemyHealthValues' and prints the index and value of every enemy whose health is above 50. Include the array declaration with at least 5 sample values.

rubric.key: Correct for loop syntax with proper bounds (Length, not hardcoded)
Output includes both index and value

questions.answer: The loop condition checks 'score < 100' but the loop body never modifies the variable 'score.' Since score remains 0 forever, the condition is always true and the loop never terminates. Fix: add a statement inside the loop body that increases score, for example 'score += 10;' after the WriteLine. This ensures score eventually reaches 100, making the condition false and terminating the loop.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies that score is never modified inside the loop
Explains why an unmodified condition causes infinite execution
Provides a correct fix that increments score inside the loop

questions.question: Write a C# class called 'Weapon' with the following: a string field 'name', an int field 'baseDamage', a float field 'critMultiplier', a constructor that takes all three values, and a method 'CalculateDamage(bool isCritical)' that returns baseDamage if not critical, or baseDamage multiplied by critMultiplier if critical.

rubric.key: Correct class declaration with three fields
Constructor properly initializes all fields from parameters
CalculateDamage method has correct signature and return type
Conditional logic correctly applies critMultiplier only when isCritical is true

questions.question: Explain what encapsulation is in object-oriented programming and describe a specific game development scenario where failing to encapsulate data leads to a bug.
questions.answer: Encapsulation is the practice of hiding an object's internal data (fields) and exposing access only through controlled methods or properties. This prevents external code from putting the object into an invalid state. Game bug scenario: A Player class has a public 'health' field. Enemy AI code directly sets player.health = player.health - damage without checking bounds. When damage exceeds current health, health goes negative (e.g., -15), and the UI displays a negative health bar. If health were encapsulated behind a TakeDamage(int amount) method, that method could clamp health to a minimum of 0 and trigger a death event, preventing the invalid negative-health state entirely.

rubric.key: Specific game scenario with cause and effect
Explains how encapsulation would prevent the bug

major_assignments.title: Console RPG Battle System
major_assignments.description: Build a text-based RPG battle system in C# that demonstrates mastery of variables, control flow, functions, and object-oriented programming. The system must support a player fighting through a sequence of enemies with turn-based combat, an inventory of usable items, and a simple leveling system.
objectives: Apply OOP principles to model game entities (Player, Enemy, Item, Weapon)
Implement a game loop that drives turn-based combat
Use functions to organize combat logic (damage calculation, status effects, inventory management)
Handle edge cases and invalid input gracefully
deliverables: Complete C# project with source code and README explaining how to build and run
At least 4 classes: Player, Enemy, Item, and one additional class of your choice
A combat loop supporting at least: Attack, Use Item, and Defend actions
At least 3 distinct enemies with different stats and at least 1 unique behavior
Input validation that handles non-numeric input, out-of-range choices, and empty inventory gracefully

criteria: Code compiles and runs without errors
Functions follow Single Responsibility Principle
Classes use encapsulation (private fields, public methods)
Consistent naming conventions and code formatting
Turn-based combat loop functions correctly
Damage calculation accounts for attack, defense, and weapon stats
Items have meaningful effects (healing, buffs, damage)
Leveling system adjusts player stats after defeating enemies
Invalid input is handled gracefully without crashing
Edge cases are covered (zero health, empty inventory, overkill damage)
Game ends properly with win and lose conditions
No infinite loops or unreachable code paths
Enemies have distinct behaviors beyond different stat values
Combat feedback is clear and informative
Additional feature beyond minimum requirements (e.g., status effects, equipment, boss fights)
README is clear and the game is genuinely playable and enjoyable
Well-structured OOP code with clean separation of concerns
Fully functional turn-based combat with meaningful player choices
Robust error handling and edge case coverage
Creative and polished game experience beyond minimum requirements

major_assignments.title: 2D Game Prototype with Game Loop and Collision
major_assignments.description: Build a simple 2D game using a game loop architecture with real-time keyboard input, AABB collision detection, and at least one game objective. The game must run at a consistent frame rate and demonstrate frame-rate-independent movement using delta time.
objectives: Implement a fixed-timestep game loop with processInput, update, and render phases
Use delta time to make movement frame-rate independent
Detect and respond to AABB collisions between game objects
Handle real-time keyboard input for player control
deliverables: Complete C# project using MonoGame, Raylib-cs, or equivalent 2D framework
Player character controlled by keyboard (WASD or arrow keys) with smooth, delta-time-based movement
At least 5 collectible objects that disappear on collision and increment a score
At least 2 obstacle objects that trigger a game-over or health loss on collision
On-screen display of score and remaining health/lives
README with build instructions and a brief description of the game's objective

criteria: Clear separation of input, update, and render phases
Fixed timestep or properly applied delta time
Game runs at stable frame rate without stuttering
Clean shutdown and resource cleanup
AABB collision detection is correctly implemented
Collisions trigger appropriate gameplay responses
No false positives or missed collisions at normal game speed
Collision logic is encapsulated in a reusable function or method
Keyboard input is responsive and lag-free
Movement is smooth and frame-rate independent
Multiple simultaneous key presses handled correctly
Input is read in the correct phase of the game loop
Clear win/lose condition communicated to the player
Score and health display are accurate and updated in real time
Game feels complete: there is a beginning, gameplay, and an ending
Additional creative touches (sound, visual feedback, difficulty progression)
Solid game loop architecture with proper delta time usage
Correct and reliable AABB collision detection
Responsive input handling with smooth player movement
Polished game experience with clear objective and feedback

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of programming concepts including syntax, logic, and problem-solving

lab_exercises.description: Weekly coding exercises completed during lab sessions, graded on correctness and code quality

console_rpg_project.description: Text-based RPG battle system demonstrating OOP, functions, and control flow

two_d_game_prototype.description: 2D game prototype demonstrating game loop architecture, collision detection, and real-time input handling

final_examination.description: Comprehensive coding exam covering all topics: data types, control flow, functions, OOP, game loops, and collision detection

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is required for all lab sessions where hands-on exercises are completed. Lecture attendance is strongly recommended. More than 3 unexcused lab absences will reduce the final grade by one half-letter.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Code that does not compile receives a maximum of 50%. Extensions require a request at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussing concepts and debugging strategies with classmates is encouraged. All submitted code must be written by you. Copying code from classmates, the internet, or AI tools without understanding and attribution constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own work. You may reference documentation, textbooks, and official tutorials. Any external code snippets used must be cited in comments. Using AI code generators for entire assignments is prohibited; using them to understand a concept is permitted with disclosure.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have access to a computer with .NET SDK 8.0+, Visual Studio Code, and a C# extension. Lab computers are available but personal laptops are recommended for homework. A keyboard is required for game input testing.
